findings.claim: This commit flips the XPU graph (cudagraph) default from opt-out (enabled when supported, resolving to PIECEWISE mode) to opt-in (disabled unless VLLM_XPU_ENABLE_XPU_GRAPH=1). In the parent snapshot, when supports_xpu_graph() was true and DP<=1, XPUPlatform.check_and_update_config kept cudagraph_mode enabled (e.g. PIECEWISE for FLASH_ATTN). After the change, a new elif branch forces compilation_config.cudagraph_mode = CUDAGraphMode.NONE for all XPU jobs that do not set the new env var. This removes CUDA/XPU graph capture+replay from the per-step decode path, reintroducing per-step kernel-launch overhead for every default XPU deployment (single-token decode latency dominated by launch cost). (1) Hot path? check_and_update_config is a one-time init/cold path, but the toggled effect lands on the per-decode-step hot path (eager kernel launches vs graph replay). (2) Caller? Invoked once at engine startup during config validation; the resulting cudagraph_mode is used every step. (3) Default enabled? The new default (VLLM_XPU_ENABLE_XPU_GRAPH=0) disables graphs for ALL XPU jobs unless explicitly opted in. Reported as a suggestion because this is an intentional, XPU-platform-scoped stability change (PR title: 'Disable xpu graph by default'); the previously-default graph path may have been unstable, so the throughput/latency forfeit is a deliberate tradeoff, not an accidental regression.
findings.suggested_benchmark: gpt-perf — config-gated regressions; run once with the feature flag at its OLD default and once at its NEW default (and once explicitly unset) — compare throughput and peak memory to detect silent path changes for jobs that never set the flag.

findings.claim: This commit is a fix (title: 'Fix slowdown in serialization'), not a regression introducer. In DynamicInferenceRequestRecord.merge(), the reconstructed DynamicInferenceRequest now carries block_size_tokens/enable_prefix_caching/precomputed_block_hashes through, and __post_init__ gains a `not self.precomputed_block_hashes` guard so it SKIPS recomputing block hashes (compute_block_hashes_batched: reshape+GPU matmul+GPU->CPU .tolist()) when they are already available from the merge. Previously merge() reconstructed the request without these fields, triggering a redundant _compute_block_hashes()/GPU sync per merged request when prefix caching is enabled. Additionally the removal of `copy.deepcopy(self.prompt_tokens)` for remaining_prompt_tokens avoids a deep copy of the prompt-token tensor on every request construction. Both changes reduce work; no new per-step hot-path cost is introduced. Correctness caveat (non-perf): merge() copies precomputed_block_hashes from requests[0] only, which is correct since prompt_tokens are identical across the merged split-request fragments.

This commit flips the default of use_aot_compile() so AOT torch.compile is enabled by default for torch>=2.10.0 (the current stable/trunk release) instead of only torch>=2.11.0.dev nightlies. Jobs on stable 2.10 that never set VLLM_USE_AOT_COMPILE now silently traverse the AOT compile/serialize/load path in vllm/compilation/decorators.py __call__. This is a COLD/startup path (first-compilation + init), not a per-step decode/forward hot path: once aot_compiled_fn is loaded it is a fast early-return, so per-step runtime is unaffected. The main risk is at warmup: the AOT path computes its cache key via aot_compile_hash_factors() (a separate helper from the normal traced-file hashing), and on source-verification mismatch it falls back to full recompilation (compile-cache-misconfig style divergence). There is a clear opt-out (VLLM_USE_AOT_COMPILE=0), and the change is an intentional feature enablement aimed at improving startup, so this is not a runtime throughput/latency regression. Flagging only as a default-enablement heads-up: verify AOT cache-key parity with the normal compile path to avoid extra first-step recompiles for the newly-default-enabled 2.10 population.
findings.suggested_benchmark: gpt-perf — config-gated regressions; run once with the feature flag at its OLD default and once at its NEW default (and once explicitly unset) — compare throughput and peak memory to detect silent path changes for jobs that never set the flag.

findings.claim: This commit changes the EP-weight-filter default from opt-out (unconditionally on for MoE+EP) to opt-in. At the parent snapshot, _init_ep_weight_filter's guard was `if not (model_config.is_moe and parallel_config.enable_expert_parallel): return`, so the immediately-preceding commit (#37136) enabled per-rank expert-weight filtering by default for every MoE+expert-parallel deployment. This diff adds a third condition `and parallel_config.enable_ep_weight_filter` (a new flag defaulting to False), so all existing MoE+EP jobs that do NOT pass `--enable-ep-weight-filter` regress: local_expert_ids stays None and each EP rank again reads the full expert weight set from disk instead of only its own shard. Three-question analysis: (1) Not a steady-state hot path — this runs once in load_weights at startup, not per-step/microbatch. (2) Caller is DefaultModelLoader.load_weights → _init_ep_weight_filter, invoked once per rank during weight loading; the filter it disables would otherwise skip non-local safetensors slices via safetensors_weights_iterator(local_expert_ids=...). (3) Default config: the perf path is now DISABLED by default, so every default MoE+EP run (DeepSeek/Mixtral/Kimi-K2.5) pays the extra startup storage I/O and per-rank host-RAM materialization that the parent avoided — cost scales with co-located EP ranks × checkpoint size. This is likely an intentional gating decision (possibly to fix compatibility of the aggressive default), and it affects only startup latency / disk I/O rather than serving throughput, hence suggestion severity.
findings.suggested_benchmark: gpt-perf — config-gated regressions; run once with the feature flag at its OLD default and once at its NEW default (and once explicitly unset) — compare throughput and peak memory to detect silent path changes for jobs that never set the flag.

findings.claim: In StructuredOutputsWorker.apply_grammar_bitmask (V2 model runner, per-decode-step hot path when structured/guided decoding is active), the new code builds the logits->bitmask mapping tensor via `torch.tensor(mapping, dtype=torch.int32, device='cpu', pin_memory=True)` on every call. Allocating + pinning a fresh host tensor each step (rather than reusing a pre-pinned staging buffer like the old UvaBufferPool round-robin pool) adds per-step host allocation + page-pinning cost. The bitmask H2D itself is now correctly on a separate copy_stream with proper wait_stream ordering (an overlap improvement), but the per-step pin_memory allocation is a small residual cost. This only fires when grammar_req_ids is non-empty (structured outputs opt-in), so it is not on the default decode path. Net effect of the commit is intended to be an improvement (async H2D on a dedicated stream); flagging only the per-step pinned-alloc as a minor cost.

In MambaMixer, the causal_conv1d_fn (prefill) and causal_conv1d_update (decode) paths now unconditionally cast xBC, conv1d weight, and conv1d bias to conv_state.dtype and cast the result back to xBC_dtype. When the new mamba-inference-conv-states-dtype flags leave the state at a dtype different from params_dtype, this materializes extra full-size copies of the conv activation on the inference hot path (per decode step / per prefill). However, the default flag value is 'bf16'; if params_dtype is also bf16 the .to() calls are no-ops (torch.Tensor.to returns self when dtype matches), so the default path incurs no new copy. The regression only bites when the user opts into a differing dtype (e.g. fp32 states) — an opt-in, not default. Weight/bias casts are small; the xBC cast scales with token_count*conv_dim.

New CLI args --mamba-inference-conv-states-dtype and --mamba-inference-ssm-states-dtype default to 'bf16'. Previously the Mamba inference conv/ssm state tensors were allocated at model_config.params_dtype (the diff replaces dtype=self.params_dtype with the new per-tensor dtypes). If a hybrid model runs inference with params_dtype=fp32/fp16 (i.e. not bf16), the new default 'bf16' silently changes the state tensor dtype vs the prior behavior, which could alter numerics/accuracy and also change the conv-kernel cast path (now casting bf16 states while activations are fp32). The mamba_states_memory sizing correctly uses per-dtype itemsize, so memory accounting is consistent, but the default 'bf16' rather than 'params_dtype' is a behavior change for non-bf16 runs.
findings.suggested_benchmark: gpt-perf — config-gated regressions; run once with the feature flag at its OLD default and once at its NEW default (and once explicitly unset) — compare throughput and peak memory to detect silent path changes for jobs that never set the flag.

findings.claim: In vllm/_custom_ops.py, onednn_mm/onednn_scaled_mm are the CPU linear path (layer.cpu_linear = lambda x,...: ops.onednn_mm(handler, x, bias)) invoked per forward for every CPU Linear layer and per-expert in cpu_fused_moe. This commit changes the handler from a cached Python int to a torch.Tensor, and the op signature now takes a Tensor. On the C++ side onednn_mm/onednn_scaled_mm call handler_tensor.item<int64_t>() on every invocation to recover the pointer. This introduces a per-call tensor.item() read (host scalar materialization) on the CPU-GEMM hot path where previously the pointer was passed directly as an int64 scalar with zero per-call overhead. For CPU inference this executes per-layer per-forward-step; item() on a tiny CPU int64 tensor is cheap but adds a dispatch + scalar-read overhead on every GEMM call rather than being a free int arg.

vllm/envs.py use_aot_compile() previously disabled AOT_COMPILE for CPU (and not current_platform.is_cpu()) per issue #32033. This commit removes that CPU guard, so on torch>=2.10.0.dev with compile-cache enabled, VLLM_USE_AOT_COMPILE now defaults to '1' on CPU too. This flips the default AOT-compile path back ON for CPU. Since the whole PR is about fixing the CPUDNNLGEMMHandler pointer being baked into the inductor artifact (the reason CPU AOT was disabled), re-enabling it is intentional and the fix addresses the root cause. Only a regression risk if the tensor-handler fix is incomplete; otherwise this restores the (faster) compiled path. Flagging as it changes a perf-feature default for all CPU jobs on new torch.
findings.suggested_benchmark: gpt-perf — config-gated regressions; run once with the feature flag at its OLD default and once at its NEW default (and once explicitly unset) — compare throughput and peak memory to detect silent path changes for jobs that never set the flag.

findings.claim: CheckpointWithoutOutput.discard_output_and_register_recompute now calls _get_share_storage(), which lazily nvcc-compiles a C++ extension via torch.utils.cpp_extension.load_inline on FIRST use. This method is on a per-layer / per-microbatch training hot path (called from transformer_layer.py input_layernorm_checkpoint / pre_mlp_norm_checkpoint, moe/experts.py activation_checkpoint, multi_latent_attention/absorbed_mla qkv_up_checkpoint — all selective-recompute paths). The extension is cached in a module global (_share_storage_ext) so the compile is paid once, not per-call, but that first compile blocks a training step for tens of seconds (nvcc invocation, temp dir build) and requires a compiler toolchain to be present at runtime. Previously the restore was a pure tensor storage resize_+copy_ (no compiler dependency, no build). This is a startup/warmup-class stall introduced into the recompute path and a new hard runtime dependency on nvcc/cpp_extension.

The change replaces the storage resize_+copy_ (a device-to-device byte copy of the recomputed activation) with a true zero-copy storage-pointer swap, which REMOVES a copy on the recompute restore path — a performance improvement, not a regression. Noting it so the copy-elimination is not mis-flagged; the only concern is the compile trigger, not the storage sharing itself.

In `_gumbel_sample_kernel` (vllm/v1/worker/gpu/sample/gumbel.py), the change unconditionally upcasts the per-token vocab logits block to FP64 (`logits = logits.to(tl.float64)`) and makes the `local_max` reduction buffer FP64, moving the widening OUTSIDE the `temp != 0.0` guard. HOT PATH: this kernel is the primary sampling path called from `Sampler.sample()` -> `gumbel_sample()` on EVERY decode step (sampler.py:173), and additionally from the eagle speculator and rejection sampler. CALLER: `Sampler.__call__/sample` is the default sampler for MRV2 — not opt-in; every request goes through it. DEFAULT-ENABLED: yes, no alternative FP32 path exists, and even greedy (temp==0) batches now pay the FP64 `tl.max` reduction over the full vocab. FP64 arithmetic is 1/2 to 1/64 the throughput of FP32 on datacenter/consumer inference GPUs (A100/H100 1/2; L40S/A10/RTX ~1/64), so the full-vocab max reduction and the `-log(-log(u))` transform (plus the extra `randint4x`+bit-ops in `tl_rand64`) run in FP64 per token per step, degrading sampling throughput for numerical-precision reasons.

The FP64 widening of the logits block is applied unconditionally before the `temp != 0.0` gumbel-noise branch. For greedy batches (temp==0), no gumbel noise is added, yet the argmax/max reduction is now done in FP64 over the full vocab and stored to an FP64 `local_max` buffer — FP64 precision is only needed for the double-log Gumbel transform, so greedy/temp==0 tokens pay FP64 reduction cost with no numerical benefit. Guarding the FP64 upcast (and local_max dtype) behind the sampling branch would avoid the regression for greedy tokens.

The commit adds a new scheduler admission gate `scheduler_reserve_full_isl` that defaults to `True` (opt-out). In the waiting-queue scheduling loop, when enabled, before admitting a new request the scheduler calls `kv_cache_manager.can_fit_full_sequence(...)`, which invokes `coordinator.get_num_blocks_to_allocate(num_tokens=full_num_tokens)` sized to the FULL input sequence length (capped at max_model_len) rather than only the first chunk. This changes default admission behavior for ALL existing jobs that never set the flag: with chunked prefill, requests that previously would be admitted (because only the first chunk needed to fit) will now be blocked until the entire ISL fits in free KV blocks. This reduces batch fullness / concurrency and can lower throughput on long-context chunked-prefill workloads, while the code comment claims it is a benefit (prevents thrashing). Whether it is net-positive is workload-dependent, but it is a default-on behavioral change on the per-step scheduler hot path. Confidence is moderate because the change may indeed improve steady-state throughput by avoiding preemption thrash; the concern is the silent default flip affecting all jobs.
findings.suggested_benchmark: gpt-perf — config-gated regressions; run once with the feature flag at its OLD default and once at its NEW default (and once explicitly unset) — compare throughput and peak memory to detect silent path changes for jobs that never set the flag.

findings.claim: `can_fit_full_sequence` calls `coordinator.get_num_blocks_to_allocate(...)` (which loops over KV cache groups / managers) for every candidate request in the waiting queue on every scheduling step, in addition to the existing `allocate_slots` call which also computes `get_num_blocks_to_allocate`. When `scheduler_reserve_full_isl` is default-True this adds a second per-request block-accounting pass per step. For a waiting queue with many requests this is O(waiting_reqs) extra coordinator work per step. Cost is modest (integer block arithmetic, not device sync) but it is default-enabled and on the per-step scheduler hot path; the gate could short-circuit or reuse the allocate_slots computation.

process_weights_after_loading() in cpu_wna16.py unconditionally calls torch.set_printoptions(profile="full", linewidth=5000, sci_mode=False) — a global side effect left over from debugging. This is on a one-time weight-loading (cold) path, not a per-step hot path, so it is not a runtime throughput/latency regression; but it globally mutates print options for the whole process as a side effect. Note: this line already exists at parent snapshot and is not introduced by this commit's diff (the diff only touches C++/gemm.h int8 brgemm gating and a new int4 kernel interface). No hot-path regression identified in the changed files.

In CPUAWQLinearMethod.process_weights_after_loading, `torch.set_printoptions(profile="full", linewidth=5000, sci_mode=False)` is called (a leftover debug statement). This runs once per quantized layer during weight loading (cold path, not per-step), so it has no runtime throughput impact, but it globally mutates torch print options as a side effect. Not a perf regression on the hot path.

The commit adds full CUDA graph support for FlexAttention by pre-building `block_mask` inside `build()` (both `_build_block_mask_direct()` and `build_block_mask()`) so that non-graph-safe ops (torch.nonzero in unique_static_unsorted, scatter_reduce, data-dependent build) run outside capture, and by copying dynamic tensors (kv_indices, kv_num_blocks, doc_ids, physical_to_logical, offset) into persistent fixed-size buffers via copy_to_persistent. This is the intended enabling change: previously `build()` produced block_mask=None and the mask was lazily built in forward()/replay. Now block_mask is unconditionally built during every `build()` call, which is on the per-step attention-metadata hot path. However, forward() already contained the same build (`if needs_rebuild_block_mask or attn_metadata.block_mask is None`), so moving it earlier into build() is essentially a relocation, not new work per step; and the persistent-buffer copies replace fresh allocations (unique_static_unsorted etc.) with copy_() into reused buffers, which is aligned with shared-workspace-reuse best practice, not a regression. The persistent buffers are one-time allocations in __init__/first build (cold path). Net: the change reduces per-step allocation and enables graph replay; no clear runtime regression. The only latent risk is that block_mask is now built even for eager (enforce_eager) paths where it may have been deferred/skipped, but since forward() built it anyway, this is neutral.

KimiK25ForConditionalGeneration.__init__ builds the vision tower and mm_projector then explicitly calls `.to(device=self.device, dtype=model_config.dtype)` on them (self.device = torch.cuda.current_device()). This is a construction-time (one-time, not per-step/per-forward) operation, so it does not sit on any decode/forward hot path. It only affects model load latency once. Whether it double-allocates depends on how weights are later loaded/sharded; other VL models in vLLM typically construct submodules directly on the target device via the init context rather than build-then-move, so this pattern can transiently materialize CPU tensors before the H2D move. Impact is limited to startup and not clearly a regression versus a baseline (this is a brand-new model file, no prior fast path being displaced).
